actinic_keratosis: Dermatoscopic image showing actinic keratosis, a rough, scaly lesion caused by chronic sun exposure.
Actinic keratosis appears as erythematous, hyperkeratotic plaques with irregular borders.
The lesion may show a sandpaper-like texture and focal areas of pigmentation or crusting.
Actinic keratosis is considered a precancerous lesion with potential to progress to squamous cell carcinoma.
This class is characterized by sun-damaged skin with superficial keratinocyte atypia.
basal_cell_carcinoma: Dermatoscopic image showing basal cell carcinoma, the most common type of skin cancer.
Basal cell carcinoma appears as a pearly or translucent nodule with telangiectasia and rolled borders.
The lesion may show ulceration, pigmentation, or arborizing blood vessels.
Basal cell carcinoma grows slowly and rarely metastasizes but can cause local tissue destruction.
This class is defined by malignant proliferation of basal cells in the epidermis.
benign_keratosis: Dermatoscopic image showing benign keratosis, a non-cancerous lesion with a warty or waxy appearance.
Benign keratosis includes seborrheic keratosis and other harmless keratotic growths.
The lesion may appear as a well-demarcated, raised plaque with a stuck-on look.
Benign keratosis is characterized by uniform color and surface texture without signs of malignancy.
This class is defined by benign proliferation of keratinocytes with no invasive features.
dermatofibroma: Dermatoscopic image showing dermatofibroma, a benign fibrous nodule commonly found on the extremities.
Dermatofibroma appears as a firm, hyperpigmented papule with a central dimple when pinched.
The lesion may show a peripheral pigment network and central white scar-like area.
Dermatofibromas are harmless and do not transform into cancer.
This class is characterized by proliferation of fibroblasts and collagen in the dermis.
melanocytic_nevus: Dermatoscopic image showing melanocytic nevus, a benign mole composed of melanocytes.
Melanocytic nevi appear as well-circumscribed, symmetric lesions with uniform color and smooth borders.
The lesion may be flat or raised and can vary in color from tan to dark brown.
Melanocytic nevi are common and usually stable over time.
This class is defined by benign proliferation of melanocytes in the skin.
melanoma: Dermatoscopic image showing melanoma, a malignant tumor of melanocytes with high metastatic potential.
Melanoma appears as an asymmetric lesion with irregular borders, multiple colors, and variegated pattern.
The lesion may show areas of regression, ulceration, or atypical pigment network.
Melanoma is aggressive and requires prompt diagnosis and treatment.
This class is characterized by malignant proliferation of atypical melanocytes.
vascular_lesion: Dermatoscopic image showing vascular lesion, such as hemangioma or angioma, composed of blood vessels.
Vascular lesions appear as red, purple, or blue papules or plaques with a spongy or compressible texture.
The lesion may blanch with pressure and show a homogeneous or lacunar pattern.
Vascular lesions are usually benign and do not show malignant features.
This class is defined by proliferation of blood vessels within the skin.
